---
title: "ANSWER KEY TO PRIVILEGED BASIS AND RELU QUIZ"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
---

# answer sheet

[Here is the answer sheet - index.qmd](./index.qmd)

# Grading rule

Each main question is worth \(2\) points.

- \(0\): absent, materially false, circular, or confuses object types;
- \(1\): correct central idea with a missing definition, assumption, or proof step;
- \(2\): correct, explicit, and type-consistent.

Equivalent formulations are acceptable.

# Part I — Measurability and information

## Answer 1

A sigma-algebra \(\mathcal F\subseteq 2^\Omega\) satisfies:

1. \(\Omega\in\mathcal F\);
2. \(E\in\mathcal F\Rightarrow E^c\in\mathcal F\);
3. \(E_n\in\mathcal F\) for all \(n\Rightarrow\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty E_n\in\mathcal F\).

A probability space is a triple

$$
(\Omega,\mathcal F,\mathbb P)
$$

where \(\mathcal F\) is a sigma-algebra and

$$
\mathbb P:\mathcal F\to[0,1]
$$

satisfies

$$
\mathbb P(\Omega)=1
$$

and countable additivity on pairwise disjoint events.

## Answer 2

For \(\mathcal G\):

- \(\Omega,\varnothing\in\mathcal G\);
- complements swap \(\{1,2\}\) and \(\{3,4\}\);
- every countable union reduces to one of the four listed sets.

Thus \(\mathcal G\) is a sigma-algebra.

For \(\mathcal H\), note

$$
\{1,2\}\cup\{1,3\}
=
\{1,2,3\}\notin\mathcal H.
$$

Therefore \(\mathcal H\) is not closed under unions and is not a sigma-algebra.

## Answer 3

\(X\) is measurable iff

$$
X^{-1}(B)\in\mathcal F
\qquad
\forall B\in\mathcal E.
$$

Preimages are used because they preserve complements and arbitrary unions:

$$
X^{-1}(B^c)
=
(X^{-1}(B))^c,
$$

$$
X^{-1}\left(\bigcup_n B_n\right)
=
\bigcup_nX^{-1}(B_n).
$$

Images do not generally preserve complements or intersections in the required way.

## Answer 4

For any \(C\in\mathcal S\),

$$
(f\circ X)^{-1}(C)
=
X^{-1}(f^{-1}(C)).
$$

Since \(f\) is measurable,

$$
f^{-1}(C)\in\mathcal E.
$$

Since \(X\) is measurable,

$$
X^{-1}(f^{-1}(C))\in\mathcal F.
$$

Hence \(f\circ X\) is measurable.

## Answer 5

The fibres of \(X\) are

$$
F_0=\{\omega_1,\omega_2\},
\qquad
F_1=\{\omega_3\},
\qquad
F_2=\{\omega_4\}.
$$

Therefore \(\sigma(X)\) consists of every union of these fibres:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma(X)=\{&
\varnothing,\Omega,
F_0,F_1,F_2,\\
&F_0\cup F_1,
F_0\cup F_2,
F_1\cup F_2
\}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Observing \(X\) distinguishes the three fibres but cannot distinguish \(\omega_1\) from \(\omega_2\).

## Answer 6

The atomic masses are

$$
\mathbb P_X(\{0\})
=
0.1+0.2
=
0.3,
$$

$$
\mathbb P_X(\{1\})
=
0.3,
$$

$$
\mathbb P_X(\{2\})
=
0.4.
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{array}{c|c}
B&\mathbb P_X(B)\\
\hline
\varnothing&0\\
\{0\}&0.3\\
\{1\}&0.3\\
\{2\}&0.4\\
\{0,1\}&0.6\\
\{0,2\}&0.7\\
\{1,2\}&0.7\\
\{0,1,2\}&1
\end{array}
$$

## Answer 7

In the real- or Euclidean-valued setting used here:

$$
Y\text{ is }\sigma(X)\text{-measurable}
\iff
\exists\text{ measurable }f\text{ such that }Y=f(X).
$$

An estimator that uses only the information contained in \(A\) must be \(\sigma(A)\)-measurable. The theorem then gives

$$
\widehat Z=g(A)
$$

for some measurable \(g\). Conversely, every measurable \(g(A)\) is \(\sigma(A)\)-measurable.

## Answer 8

1. \(A\) is a measurable map \(\Omega\to\mathbb R^d\).
2. \(a\) is a deterministic vector in \(\mathbb R^d\).
3. \(\sigma(A)\) is a collection of subsets of \(\Omega\), specifically a sub-sigma-algebra of \(\mathcal F\).
4. \(\{A\in B\}=A^{-1}(B)\) is an event in \(\mathcal F\).
5. \(\mathbb E[Z\mid A]\) is a random vector, defined up to almost-sure equality, and is \(\sigma(A)\)-measurable.

## Answer 9

The map

$$
z\mapsto Qz
$$

is linear and continuous, hence Borel measurable.

Coordinatewise ReLU

$$
r(y)_i=\max(0,y_i)
$$

is continuous, hence Borel measurable.

Since \(Z\) is measurable and compositions of measurable maps are measurable,

$$
A=r\circ Q\circ Z
$$

is measurable.

# Part II — Conditional expectation

## Answer 10

A conditional expectation \(Y=\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]\) satisfies:

1. \(Y\) is \(\mathcal G\)-measurable;
2. \(Y\in L^1\);
3. for every \(G\in\mathcal G\),
   $$
   \mathbb E[X1_G]
   =
   \mathbb E[Y1_G].
   $$

It is unique up to almost-sure equality.

## Answer 11

The two atoms of \(\mathcal G\) are

$$
G_1=\{1,2\},
\qquad
G_2=\{3,4\}.
$$

On \(G_1\),

$$
\mathbb E[X\mid G_1]
=
\frac{0.1(2)+0.2(-1)}{0.1+0.2}
=
0.
$$

On \(G_2\),

$$
\mathbb E[X\mid G_2]
=
\frac{0.3(4)+0.4(0)}{0.3+0.4}
=
\frac{12}{7}.
$$

Hence

$$
\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]
=
\left(0,0,\frac{12}{7},\frac{12}{7}\right).
$$

## Answer 12

The events in \(\mathcal G\) are

$$
\varnothing,\Omega,G_1,G_2.
$$

For \(\varnothing\), both sides are zero.

For \(G_1\),

$$
\mathbb E[X1_{G_1}]
=
0.1(2)+0.2(-1)
=
0,
$$

while

$$
\mathbb E[\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]1_{G_1}]
=
0.
$$

For \(G_2\),

$$
\mathbb E[X1_{G_2}]
=
1.2,
$$

while

$$
\mathbb E[\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]1_{G_2}]
=
(0.3+0.4)\frac{12}{7}
=
1.2.
$$

For \(\Omega\), add the two block identities.

## Answer 13

\(X\) itself is \(\mathcal G\)-measurable and integrable. For every \(G\in\mathcal G\),

$$
\mathbb E[X1_G]
=
\mathbb E[X1_G].
$$

Thus \(X\) satisfies the defining properties of \(\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]\). By almost-sure uniqueness,

$$
\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]=X
\quad\text{a.s.}
$$

## Answer 14

The constant \(\mathbb E[X]\) is \(\mathcal G\)-measurable. For \(G\in\mathcal G\), independence gives

$$
\mathbb E[X1_G]
=
\mathbb E[X]\mathbb E[1_G]
=
\mathbb E[X]\mathbb P(G).
$$

Also,

$$
\mathbb E[\mathbb E[X]1_G]
=
\mathbb E[X]\mathbb P(G).
$$

Hence the constant \(\mathbb E[X]\) satisfies the defining property.

## Answer 15

Let

$$
Y=\mathbb E[\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]\mid\mathcal H].
$$

Then \(Y\) is \(\mathcal H\)-measurable.

For every \(H\in\mathcal H\), since \(H\in\mathcal G\),

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb E[Y1_H]
&=
\mathbb E[\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G]1_H]\\
&=
\mathbb E[X1_H].
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus \(Y\) satisfies the defining property of \(\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal H]\). Uniqueness gives

$$
Y=\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal H]
\quad\text{a.s.}
$$

## Answer 16

Let

$$
m=\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G].
$$

Write

$$
X-\mathbb E[X]
=
(X-m)+(m-\mathbb E[X]).
$$

Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Var}(X)
&=
\mathbb E[(X-m)^2]
+
\mathbb E[(m-\mathbb E[X])^2]\\
&\quad+
2\mathbb E[(X-m)(m-\mathbb E[X])].
\end{aligned}
$$

The cross term is zero because \(m-\mathbb E[X]\) is \(\mathcal G\)-measurable:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb E[(X-m)(m-\mathbb E[X])]
&=
\mathbb E[
\mathbb E[(X-m)(m-\mathbb E[X])\mid\mathcal G]
]\\
&=
\mathbb E[
(m-\mathbb E[X])
\mathbb E[X-m\mid\mathcal G]
]\\
&=0.
\end{aligned}
$$

Also,

$$
\mathbb E[(X-m)^2]
=
\mathbb E[\operatorname{Var}(X\mid\mathcal G)],
$$

and

$$
\mathbb E[(m-\mathbb E[X])^2]
=
\operatorname{Var}(m).
$$

## Answer 17

Let

$$
m=\mathbb E[Z\mid\mathcal G],
\qquad
\mu=\mathbb E[Z].
$$

Use

$$
Z-\mu=(Z-m)+(m-\mu)
$$

and expand the outer product. The cross terms vanish because

$$
\mathbb E[Z-m\mid\mathcal G]=0.
$$

Thus

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Cov}(Z)
&=
\mathbb E[(Z-m)(Z-m)^T]
+
\mathbb E[(m-\mu)(m-\mu)^T]\\
&=
\mathbb E[\operatorname{Cov}(Z\mid\mathcal G)]
+
\operatorname{Cov}(m).
\end{aligned}
$$

Expectation of a random matrix is taken entrywise.

## Answer 18

By definition,

$$
\operatorname{Cov}(Z\mid\mathcal G)
=
\mathbb E[(Z-m)(Z-m)^T\mid\mathcal G].
$$

Using linearity of trace and conditional expectation,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{tr}(\operatorname{Cov}(Z\mid\mathcal G))
&=
\mathbb E[
\operatorname{tr}((Z-m)(Z-m)^T)
\mid\mathcal G
]\\
&=
\mathbb E[\|Z-m\|^2\mid\mathcal G].
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking expectations and applying the tower property gives

$$
\mathbb E[
\operatorname{tr}(\operatorname{Cov}(Z\mid\mathcal G))
]
=
\mathbb E\|Z-m\|^2.
$$

## Answer 19

If \(A>0\), then \(Z=A\).

If \(A=0\), then \(Z\in[-1,0]\), and conditionally \(Z\) is uniform on \([-1,0]\). Therefore

$$
\mathbb E[Z\mid A=0]
=
-\frac12,
$$

and

$$
\operatorname{Var}(Z\mid A=0)
=
\frac{1}{12}.
$$

Hence

$$
h(a)
=
\begin{cases}
-\frac12,&a=0,\\
a,&a>0.
\end{cases}
$$

The conditional variance is

$$
\operatorname{Var}(Z\mid A)
=
\begin{cases}
\frac1{12},&A=0,\\
0,&A>0.
\end{cases}
$$

Since \(\mathbb P(A=0)=1/2\),

$$
\operatorname{MMSE}
=
\frac12\cdot\frac1{12}
=
\frac1{24}.
$$

## Answer 20

If \(A>0\), then \(Z=A\).

If \(A=0\), then \(Z\le0\), so

$$
\mathbb E[Z\mid A=0]
=
-\sqrt{\frac2\pi}.
$$

Thus

$$
h(a)
=
\begin{cases}
-\sqrt{\frac2\pi},&a=0,\\
a,&a>0.
\end{cases}
$$

All conditional error occurs on \(A=0\). Therefore

$$
\operatorname{MMSE}
=
\mathbb P(Z\le0)
\operatorname{Var}(Z\mid Z\le0)
=
\frac12\left(1-\frac2\pi\right).
$$

## Answer 21

For continuously distributed \(A\),

$$
\mathbb P(A=a)=0
$$

for each fixed \(a\), so the elementary ratio divides by zero.

The primary object is instead

$$
\mathbb E[Z\mid\sigma(A)],
$$

defined by measurability and integral identities. Under standard regularity conditions it has a version

$$
h(A),
$$

and one may use \(h(a)\) as a version of \(\mathbb E[Z\mid A=a]\). Values of \(h\) on sets of \(A\)-measure zero are arbitrary.

## Answer 22

Since \(Z\ge0\) almost surely,

$$
A=\operatorname{ReLU}(Z)=Z
\qquad\text{a.s.}
$$

Therefore

$$
\mathbb E[Z\mid A]
=
A
=
Z
\qquad\text{a.s.}
$$

and the MMSE is zero.

# Part III — \(L^2\) geometry and MMSE

## Answer 23

$$
L^2(\Omega,\mathcal F,\mathbb P)
=
\{X:\Omega\to\mathbb R:
X\text{ measurable and }\mathbb E[X^2]<\infty\}/\!\sim
$$

where

$$
X\sim Y
\iff
X=Y\quad\text{a.s.}
$$

The inner product and norm are

$$
\langle X,Y\rangle
=
\mathbb E[XY],
$$

$$
\|X\|_{L^2}
=
\sqrt{\mathbb E[X^2]}.
$$

If \(X=Y\) a.s., then

$$
\|X-Y\|_{L^2}=0.
$$

A normed space must identify zero-distance objects.

## Answer 24

$$
L^2(\Omega,\mathcal G,\mathbb P)
$$

is the set of square-integrable \(\mathcal G\)-measurable random variables modulo a.s. equality.

If \(X,Y\) are \(\mathcal G\)-measurable, then

$$
aX+bY
$$

is \(\mathcal G\)-measurable for \(a,b\in\mathbb R\), and remains square-integrable. Hence it is a linear subspace.

The projection theorem requires the subspace to be **closed**. This subspace is indeed closed in \(L^2\).

## Answer 25

Let \(M\) be a nonempty closed linear subspace of a Hilbert space \(H\). For each \(x\in H\), there is a unique \(m\in M\) such that

$$
\|x-m\|
=
\inf_{v\in M}\|x-v\|.
$$

This \(m=P_Mx\) is equivalently characterized by

$$
x-m\perp M,
$$

meaning

$$
\langle x-m,v\rangle=0
\qquad
\forall v\in M.
$$

## Answer 26

Let

$$
m=\mathbb E[X\mid\mathcal G].
$$

For \(V=1_G\), \(G\in\mathcal G\), the defining property gives

$$
\mathbb E[(X-m)1_G]=0.
$$

By linearity, this holds for every \(\mathcal G\)-measurable simple function \(V\).

For general \(V\in L^2(\mathcal G)\), choose simple \(V_n\to V\) in \(L^2\). Since \(X-m\in L^2\), Cauchy–Schwarz gives

$$
|\mathbb E[(X-m)(V-V_n)]|
\le
\|X-m\|_2\|V-V_n\|_2
\to0.
$$

Since each

$$
\mathbb E[(X-m)V_n]=0,
$$

the limit is zero.

## Answer 27

Write

$$
Z-T=(Z-m)+(m-T).
$$

Therefore

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|Z-T\|^2
&=
\|Z-m\|^2+\|m-T\|^2\\
&\quad+2(Z-m)^T(m-T).
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking expectations, the cross term is

$$
\mathbb E[(Z-m)^T(m-T)].
$$

The vector \(m-T\) is \(\mathcal G\)-measurable, while

$$
\mathbb E[Z-m\mid\mathcal G]=0.
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb E[(Z-m)^T(m-T)]
&=
\mathbb E[
\mathbb E[(Z-m)^T(m-T)\mid\mathcal G]
]\\
&=
\mathbb E[
\mathbb E[Z-m\mid\mathcal G]^T(m-T)
]\\
&=0.
\end{aligned}
$$

The decomposition follows.

## Answer 28

Let

$$
\mathcal G=\sigma(A).
$$

Every admissible estimator using only \(A\) is a square-integrable \(\mathcal G\)-measurable random vector and therefore has the form \(g(A)\), up to a.s. equality.

Set

$$
m=\mathbb E[Z\mid A].
$$

Question 27 gives

$$
\mathbb E\|Z-g(A)\|^2
=
\mathbb E\|Z-m\|^2
+
\mathbb E\|m-g(A)\|^2.
$$

The second term is nonnegative and is zero for \(g(A)=m\). Therefore

$$
\inf_g
\mathbb E\|Z-g(A)\|^2
=
\mathbb E\|Z-m\|^2.
$$

The minimizer is unique as an element of \(L^2\), hence unique up to almost-sure equality.

If a candidate \(g(A)\) is not square-integrable, its squared risk is infinite or not an admissible finite-risk competitor.

## Answer 29

Let

$$
m=\mathbb E[Z\mid A].
$$

Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(Q)
&=
\mathbb E\|Z-m\|^2\\
&=
\mathbb E[
\mathbb E[\|Z-m\|^2\mid A]
]\\
&=
\mathbb E[
\operatorname{tr}(
\mathbb E[(Z-m)(Z-m)^T\mid A]
)
]\\
&=
\mathbb E[
\operatorname{tr}(\operatorname{Cov}(Z\mid A))
].
\end{aligned}
$$

The second equality is the tower property. The third uses

$$
\|x\|^2=\operatorname{tr}(xx^T).
$$

## Answer 30

Since

$$
L(Q)
=
\mathbb E\|Z-\mathbb E[Z\mid A]\|^2,
$$

we have \(L(Q)=0\) iff

$$
Z=\mathbb E[Z\mid A]
\qquad\text{a.s.}
$$

The right-hand side is \(\sigma(A)\)-measurable, so this is equivalent to \(Z\) being \(\sigma(A)\)-measurable up to a.s. equality.

By the factorization theorem, this is equivalent to the existence of measurable \(g\) with

$$
Z=g(A)
\qquad\text{a.s.}
$$

This only requires recovery on a set carrying probability one. The deterministic observation map may fail to be injective elsewhere, including on points or sets that the law of \(Z\) never visits.

## Answer 31

The unrestricted class contains every measurable square-integrable function of \(A\). The affine class contains only

$$
BA+b.
$$

The unrestricted optimum is

$$
\mathbb E[Z\mid A].
$$

Example: let \(A\sim\mathcal N(0,1)\) and define

$$
Z=A^2.
$$

Then \(Z\) is already known from \(A\), so

$$
\mathbb E[Z\mid A]=A^2
$$

and the unrestricted MMSE is zero.

No affine function \(bA+c\) equals \(A^2\) almost surely under a nondegenerate Gaussian law. Hence the best affine risk is strictly positive.

## Answer 32

Let

$$
Y=QZ.
$$

Since \(Z\sim\mathcal N(0,I_d)\) and \(Q\) is orthogonal,

$$
Y\sim\mathcal N(0,I_d).
$$

Also,

$$
Z=Q^TY
$$

and

$$
A=\operatorname{ReLU}(Y).
$$

For any decoder \(g\), define

$$
h(a)=Qg(a).
$$

This is a bijection between measurable decoders \(g\) and measurable decoders \(h\). Since \(Q\) preserves norms,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|Z-g(A)\|
&=
\|QZ-Qg(A)\|\\
&=
\|Y-h(\operatorname{ReLU}(Y))\|.
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore

$$
L(Q)
=
\inf_h
\mathbb E\|
Y-h(\operatorname{ReLU}(Y))
\|^2.
$$

The right-hand side depends only on the distribution of \(Y\), which is \(\mathcal N(0,I_d)\) for every \(Q\). Hence \(L(Q)\) is constant.

# Bridge problems

## Bridge 1

If \(Z\ge0\) coordinatewise almost surely, then

$$
\operatorname{ReLU}(Z)=Z
$$

almost surely. At \(Q=I\),

$$
A=Z,
$$

so \(g(a)=a\) reconstructs \(Z\) exactly. Thus \(L(I)=0\).

Sparsity and the exponential amplitude distribution are irrelevant because only nonnegativity is used.

## Bridge 2

The collision argument is incomplete because:

1. the two points may both have probability zero;
2. one or both points may lie outside the support of \(Z\);
3. the collision may occur only on a null subset of latent space;
4. the conditional law given the shared observation may still be degenerate almost surely;
5. for continuous distributions, pointwise collisions do not by themselves establish positive conditional variance on a positive-probability set of observations.

The required claim is probabilistic, not merely set-theoretic.

## Bridge 3

Since

$$
L(Q)
=
\mathbb E[
\operatorname{tr}(\operatorname{Cov}(Z\mid A))
],
$$

and the integrand is nonnegative,

$$
L(Q)>0
$$

iff

$$
\operatorname{tr}(\operatorname{Cov}(Z\mid A))>0
$$

with positive probability.

A concrete sufficient condition is: there exists a measurable set \(B\) in observation space with

$$
\mathbb P(A\in B)>0
$$

and a constant \(\varepsilon>0\) such that

$$
\operatorname{tr}(\operatorname{Cov}(Z\mid A))
\ge\varepsilon
$$

almost surely on \(\{A\in B\}\). Then

$$
L(Q)\ge
\varepsilon\,\mathbb P(A\in B)>0.
$$

The uniform lower bound can be weakened; it is enough that the nonnegative conditional trace be positive on a set of positive probability.

## Bridge 4

Here

$$
QZ
=
\frac1{\sqrt2}
\begin{bmatrix}
Z_1-Z_2\\
Z_1+Z_2
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

Since \(Z_1,Z_2\ge0\),

$$
A_2=\frac{Z_1+Z_2}{\sqrt2}.
$$

Also,

$$
A_1
=
\frac{(Z_1-Z_2)_+}{\sqrt2}.
$$

On the region

$$
0<Z_1<Z_2,
$$

we observe

$$
A_1=0,
\qquad
A_2=\frac{Z_1+Z_2}{\sqrt2}.
$$

Thus the observation reveals the sum but not the split.

A rigorous positivity proof can proceed by:

1. restricting to the event that both coordinates are active;
2. identifying the joint density on \(0<z_1<z_2\);
3. changing variables to
   $$
   s=z_1+z_2,
   \qquad
   u=z_1;
   $$
4. showing that, conditional on a nontrivial interval of sums \(s\), the variable \(u\) has a nondegenerate conditional density on
   $$
   0<u<s/2;
   $$
5. concluding that
   $$
   \operatorname{Var}(Z_1\mid A)>0
   $$
   on a positive-probability set of observations;
6. hence
   $$
   \operatorname{tr}(\operatorname{Cov}(Z\mid A))>0
   $$
   there and \(L(Q)>0\).

The main technical issue is that conditioning on an exact continuous value of the sum requires a regular conditional distribution or a density calculation. Merely drawing the fibre is not enough.
